title: Hosea 3
subtitle: Hosea Redeems His Wife

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Hosea Redeems Gomer

sections.header: Verses 1-3 — "Go, Show Your Love to Your Wife Again"

heb.paragraph: The verb appears three times in v. 1: God commands Hosea to 'love' his wife, just as YHWH 'loves' the Israelites, even though they 'love' raisin cakes offered to other gods. The triple use creates a theological triangle: divine love for Israel, prophetic love for Gomer, and Israel's misdirected love for idolatrous ritual foods. Hosea's love for Gomer must mirror YHWH's for Israel: persistent despite betrayal.

refs.note: Thirty shekels as the price of a slave, roughly equivalent to Hosea's redemption payment for Gomer.
Zechariah's thirty pieces of silver thrown to the potter, echoing the slave-price of redemption and anticipating the price of Jesus' betrayal.
Paul's theology of Christ 'buying back' (redeeming) those under the law's curse extends the Hosea-Gomer redemption pattern.

notes.note: The command to love an adulterous wife a second time is perhaps the clearest Old Testament picture of God's grace. Gomer has not repented or changed; the love is commanded despite her ongoing unfaithfulness. This is not reactive mercy (responding to repentance) but proactive grace (pursuing the unfaithful).

The redemption price suggests Gomer had been sold into slavery or was being kept by a lover who demanded payment for her release. The combination of silver and barley indicates limited financial resources, adding a deeply human dimension to the prophetic sign-act.

The period of required fidelity before full restoration mirrors the theological pattern of discipline before blessing. Israel must pass through a period of deprivation before the relationship is fully renewed.

Calvin saw the command as a supreme test of obedience: to love where love is not deserved, to redeem what has been freely given away. The prophet's compliance demonstrates the principle that divine commands are to be obeyed even when they confound human reason.

The price paid in barley rather than silver alone suggested to Calvin a deliberate humbling: barley was the food of the poor and of animals. The redemption is real but costly, and the medium of payment underscores the degradation from which Gomer is being rescued.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase 'love raisin cakes' (ohave ashishey anavim) refers to raisin cakes used in Canaanite fertility rituals (cf. Isa 16:7; Jer 7:18). The cakes were associated with cultic feasting in honor of Baal or Asherah.

The combination of silver and barley to make up the purchase price (15 shekels + 1.5 homers of barley) totals approximately 30 shekels, the standard price of a slave in ancient Israel (Exod 21:32). This suggests Gomer had been reduced to slave status.

stuart.source: Douglas Stuart, Hosea-Jonah, WBC (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Stuart argues that the chapter narrates a distinct event from chapters 1-2, occurring later in the marriage after Gomer has left Hosea and fallen into poverty or slavery. The word 'od ('again/still') indicates continuity: the same love that initiated the marriage now pursues the wife who has abandoned it. The sign-act is complete only when the prophet not only marries but also redeems his unfaithful wife.

The probationary period ('you are to live with me many days') parallels the period of exile that Israel must endure before restoration. The sexual abstinence requirement may reflect purification laws or simply the requirement that trust be rebuilt before intimacy resumes.

andersen_freedman.source: Francis I. Andersen & David Noel Freedman, Anchor Bible — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: This passage is the biographical counterpart to the oracular restoration of 2:14-23. What the poetry promises in cosmic terms, the narrative enacts in domestic terms. The purchase price, with its echo of slave valuation, anticipates the redemption theology that will become central to both prophetic and apostolic thought. The period of imposed fidelity (v. 3) is Hosea's version of the wilderness sojourn: a liminal period between judgment and full restoration.

hist.context: This brief chapter shifts to first-person narration, with Hosea himself as speaker. The command to love 'again' (or 'another,' depending on translation) implies a period of separation during which Gomer had been living with another man or had fallen into slavery. The redemption price is significant: the combination of silver and barley totals approximately thirty shekels — the price of a slave (Exod 21:32) and, centuries later, the price paid for Jesus (Zech 11:12-13; Matt 26:15). The period of enforced waiting (v. 3) may reflect a purification requirement or a probationary period before full conjugal rights are restored. This is the last narrative section of the book; chapters 4-14 shift entirely to oracular poetry.

heb.gloss: afterward / in the end of days
heb.paragraph: This temporal marker signals eschatological hope. The waiting period described in v. 4 is not permanent but transitional. The phrase connects to the broader prophetic theme of the 'latter days' (beʾaharit hayyamim), pointing beyond the immediate exile to an ultimate restoration. The return to YHWH and 'David their king' introduces messianic expectation into the Hosean corpus.

refs.note: Jeremiah echoes Hosea: 'They will serve the LORD their God and David their king, whom I will raise up for them.'
Ezekiel's vision of 'my servant David' as the one shepherd over reunited Israel develops Hosea's brief allusion.
The angel's announcement that Jesus will inherit 'the throne of his father David' fulfills the Hosean/prophetic hope.

notes.note: The list of things Israel will lack encompasses both true worship (sacrifice, ephod) and false worship (sacred stones, household gods). The exile strips Israel of every religious prop, leaving them naked before God. This comprehensive deprivation is the national equivalent of Gomer's imposed probation.

The mention of 'David their king' is a messianic prophecy. Since the Davidic line did not rule the north, this looks forward to the messianic reunion of all Israel under one king. The phrase 'trembling to the LORD and to his blessings in the last days' points to an eschatological fulfillment in Christ.

Calvin noted that the deprivation period is pedagogical: God strips away every source of false security so that Israel is forced to seek him alone. The return 'in the last days' signifies that the full realization of this promise awaits the coming of Christ and the gathering of the church from all nations.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The term massebah ('sacred stone/pillar') and teraphim ('household gods') are associated with Canaanite worship. Their inclusion alongside legitimate religious objects (sacrifice, ephod) indicates that Israel's religion had become an inseparable mixture of Yahwistic and pagan elements.

stuart.source: Douglas Stuart, Hosea-Jonah, WBC (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Stuart understands the 'many days' as the period of exile and diaspora, during which Israel would be stripped of both state and cult. The reference to David is not nostalgic but eschatological: Hosea anticipates a future reunification under a Davidic ruler that transcends the historical division of the kingdoms.

andersen_freedman.source: Francis I. Andersen & David Noel Freedman, Anchor Bible — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The enumeration in v. 4 is remarkable for its mixing of categories: king and prince (political), sacrifice and sacred stone (cultic, both legitimate and illegitimate), ephod and teraphim (oracular). The message is that the entire apparatus of national life — political, religious, and divinatory — will be suspended. Verse 5 then introduces the concept of return (shuv), which becomes the dominant motif of chapters 4-14.

hist.context: The application to Israel (vv. 4-5) interprets the Gomer narrative allegorically. The 'many days' without king, prince, sacrifice, sacred stone, ephod, or household gods describes the condition of exile: stripped of all the institutions — both legitimate (monarchy, sacrifice, ephod) and illegitimate (sacred stones, household gods) — that defined Israelite national life. This is the probationary period of v. 3 writ large. The mention of 'David their king' is remarkable coming from a northern prophet, since the northern kingdom had rejected the Davidic dynasty. It suggests that Hosea recognized the Davidic covenant as the legitimate channel of messianic hope, transcending the north-south divide.

rows.label: vv.1
rows.text: Divine command: love the adulteress again

rows.label: vv.5
rows.text: Eschatological hope: return to YHWH and David

lit.note: This tightly constructed chapter moves from command (v. 1) to compliance (v. 2) to condition (v. 3) to application (vv. 4-5). It is the shortest chapter in the prophetic corpus but arguably the most theologically dense, encapsulating redemption, probation, and eschatological hope in five verses. It closes the biographical section (chs. 1-3) before the shift to oracles (chs. 4-14).

themes.note: Mercy reaches its maximum score in this chapter: the redemption of Gomer despite her adultery is the most vivid Old Testament picture of unmerited grace. Covenant remains dominant because the entire chapter is about covenant renewal after covenant violation.

hebtext.note: The verb appears three times in v. 1: God commands Hosea to 'love' his wife, just as YHWH 'loves' the Israelites, even though they 'love' raisin cakes offered to other gods. The triple use creates a theological triangle: divine love for Israel, prophetic love for Gomer, and Israel's misdirected love for idolatrous ritual foods. Hosea's love for Gomer must mirror YHWH's for Israel: persistent despite betrayal.

trans.title: Comparing translations in Hosea Redeems His Wife

translations.text: The Lord said to me, “Go, show your love to your wife again, though she is loved by another man and is an adulteress. Love her as the Lord loves the Israelites, though they turn to other gods and love the sacred raisin cakes.”

And the LORD said to me, “Go again, love a woman who is loved by another man and is an adulteress, even as the LORD loves the children of Israel, though they turn to other gods and love cakes of raisins.”
Afterward the Israelites will return and seek the Lord their God and David their king. They will come trembling to the Lord and to his blessings in the last days.
Afterward the children of Israel shall return and seek the LORD their God, and David their king, and they shall come in fear to the LORD and to his goodness in the latter days.

Zechariah's thirty pieces of silver thrown to the potter, echoing the slave-price of redemption and anticipating the price of Jesus' betrayal.
Paul's theology of Christ 'buying back' (redeeming) those under the law's curse extends the Hosea-Gomer redemption pattern.

coaching.tip: 'Go again, love a woman who is loved by another man and is an adulteress' (v. 1). This is Gomer — Hosea must buy back his own wife from slavery or prostitution. The price (15 shekels + barley) is half a slave's value. She's been reduced to almost nothing. Yet Hosea redeems her. This is the gospel in miniature.

prayer_prompt: As Hosea buys back his adulterous wife at the price of a slave, consider asking God what it cost Him to redeem you — and whether you live as someone who has been purchased or someone who is still for sale.
